content:
The **train vs inference** split predates deep learning. Classical **least-squares regression** and **logistic regression** were fit on labeled samples (training) then applied to new observations (prediction) — the same two-phase pattern scikit-learn later named **fit** and **predict**.

**Neural network** research in the 1980s formalized **forward** and **backward** passes: training required both; deployment used forward-only inference on fixed weights.

The **1990s–2000s** ML industry scaled **batch training** on warehouse data and **batch scoring** jobs — nightly model refresh plus daytime inference without retraining.

**Web-scale serving** (search ads, recommender systems) drove **online inference** infrastructure: low-latency predict paths separate from offline training pipelines.

The **2010s deep learning** era added GPU training clusters and **model export** (ONNX, TensorRT, TFLite) to run the same trained graph on phones and edge devices.

Today **MLOps** platforms treat training jobs and inference endpoints as distinct lifecycle stages — versioning, monitoring, and rollback apply primarily to the inference surface while training runs as scheduled experiments.

timeline.label: Classical statistics (early 1900s)
timeline.description: Fit parametric models on labeled samples; predict on new observations — conceptual train/infer split.

timeline.label: Backpropagation era (1980s)
timeline.description: Neural training uses forward + backward passes; inference is forward-only on learned weights.

timeline.label: scikit-learn fit/predict (2007+)
timeline.description: Standard Python API names training (fit) and inference (predict) explicitly across estimators.

timeline.label: Batch scoring at scale (2000s)
timeline.description: Enterprises separate overnight training from daytime batch inference on millions of rows.

timeline.label: MLOps & model serving (2010s–present)
timeline.description: Dedicated inference endpoints, A/B model versions, and export formats for edge and cloud serving.
